title: Super tax change threatens to ignite family feuds
full_text: Other Editions 24 April 2026 Wealth Super tax change threatens to ignite family feuds theaustralian.com.au Bianca Hartge-Hazelman 22 April 2026 19:00 GMT 889 words English AUSTOL © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. The most significant superannuation tax change in a decade could create messy inheritances among the nation’s wealthiest families, potentially forcing children in blended families to foot the tax bill for a stepparent’s windfall. SMSF Alliance principal David Busoli said the new Division 296 legislation, which targets about 90,000 Australians with superannuation balances over $3m and officially kicks off on July 1, creates an “unintended mess” for executors and estate beneficiaries. “It is not that unusual for a second spouse to keep the superannuation benefits and for the kids from the previous marriages to get more of the estate,” Mr Busoli said. “The simple reason for that is superannuation benefits payable to the spouse are tax-free whereas they’re probably going to be hit with 15 per cent of the taxable component to the kids.” Division 296 applies to super balances above $3m. However, should the superannuant die, it is the estate that must fund the tax bill created by the new super tax. This means a spouse could collect the super, tax-free, while the step-kids need to pay the tax due from the estate, setting the scene for some nasty inheritance fights. While the Australian Bureau of Statistics reports that roughly 12 per cent of Australian households are blended, this figure only counts families with children still living at home. For the Baby Boomer generation, grey divorce rates are on the rise, with couples calling it quits in one in three first-time marriages. “In my 40 years of practice, I would estimate that one out of the three families I see are now blended, which is much higher than 12 per cent,” said Coote Family Lawyers head of estate planning Suzanne Jones. With the $3.2 trillion intergenerational wealth transfer under way, the presence of blended families plus the new super tax creates the makings of a highly litigious cocktail. “It’s often the case with blended family estates that there can be no love lost between the new stepparent and the kids from the previous marriage,” Mr Busoli said. “Because superannuation often passes outside the will via a binding death benefit nomination, the person receiving the money may not be the person responsible for the Division 296 tax,” he said. “It is conceivable that a significant tax liability could arise against the estate after it has been distributed, thereby placing an executor in a difficult position.” Of course, estate planning lawyers and financial planners will try to take this possibility into account, but doing so won’t be easy. Division 296 could create a spike in liability for those winding up wealthy estates because it in effect creates a death tax. The tax payable is worked out based on the higher of the balance at the start of the financial year and the balance at the end of the year. This means if a person dies in January there could be six months’ tax due. From July, the limit on how much superannuation an individual can shift into a tax-free pension, known as the transfer balance cap, is set to rise from $2m to $2.1m. Under Australian law, when a member dies, any superannuation balance exceeding the surviving spouse’s transfer balance cap must be paid out of the superannuation system. If a self-managed superannuation fund is “asset-rich but cash-poor”, the trustee is often forced to sell assets to meet the mandatory payout. Mr Busoli said any estate planning modifications must consider two things: To be flexible enough to deal with whatever number is generated at the time and for it to be enforceable. “We are moving into a reality where the children of a first marriage may effectively ‘subsidise’ the tax-free superannuation windfall of a stepparent,” he said. “Unless a will is specifically drafted to create an indemnity or an adjustment clause and the spouse actually has an interest in the estate to adjust, executors will be left presiding over a family legal battle.” The “unintended mess” is compounded by a lag in tax administration. In many cases, a Division 296 assessment may not be issued by the ATO until months after the estate assets have been distributed. If an executor has already cleared the estate’s bank accounts to pay the children, they may find themselves personally liable for a debt they have no funds left to cover. Ms Jones said estate planning lawyers would have to be very aware of what the net outcome from spouses and beneficiaries of the estate would be. “They will need to consider if adjustments are needed to take into account the tax payable on the super death benefits and embed a formula in the will to accommodate any adjustments,” she said. “It may also mean that distribution of estates has to be delayed while the tax is assessed and or where the estate and superannuation are to be divided between a surviving spouse and children of another relationship. “The super may have to be directed to the estate with a well-drafted clause empowering the executors to firstly apply as much of the superannuation death benefit as is possible to satisfy the entitlement of the surviving spouse who will bear the lesser tax burden.” Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: A new superannuation tax change, effective July 1, targets Australians with super balances over $3 million, potentially complicating inheritance for blended families. The legislation may lead to tax liabilities that fall on children from previous marriages, creating disputes among beneficiaries. Estate planning will need to adapt to these changes to avoid legal conflicts and ensure fair distribution of assets.

key_points: Division 296 legislation imposes a tax on super balances over $3 million, affecting inheritance dynamics.
Blended families may face disputes as children from previous marriages could be liable for taxes on a stepparent's superannuation benefits.
Estate planning must incorporate strategies to address potential tax liabilities and prevent executor liability.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights the need for updated estate planning strategies in light of new tax regulations affecting superannuation.
